evals.prompt: We've been iterating on our customer support agent for 3 months. It handles returns, billing questions, and escalations. We've refined the system prompt a dozen times and we're stuck at about 78% on our eval set. My team is pushing to fine-tune the model. Is that the right call, or are we missing something in the harness?
expectations: Recommends the model-swap experiment before committing to fine-tuning: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same harness and check whether scores improve materially

Frames the decision correctly: if scores jump on model swap, the bottleneck is the model (SFT is warranted); if scores don't improve, the bottleneck is still the harness (context quality, tool design, prompt structure)
Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is 10–100× more expensive than harness iteration and harder to reverse — exhaust the harness first
Identifies what SFT is appropriate for in this context: stabilizing output format and baking in protocol knowledge, not injecting factual knowledge about return policies (that belongs in context)
Avoids recommending RL at this stage — RL is warranted only when SFT has hit its ceiling and deployment distribution shifts systematically, neither of which is established here

evals.prompt: should we use SFT or RL to train our coding agent? it needs to handle novel codebases it's never seen before. can handle novel codebases it's never seen before. we have about 2000 labeled examples of good code edits
expectations: Applies the one-sentence rule: SFT memorizes (fixed input→output mapping from demonstrations), RL generalizes (transferable strategy by optimizing for outcomes)
Identifies novel codebases as a distribution shift problem — the classic SFT failure mode — making RL the appropriate tool for generalization beyond the training distribution
Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must precede RL for smaller models with structured output requirements — 2000 examples may be sufficient to stabilize format before RL
Addresses demonstration quality: 2000 examples is a reasonable SFT dataset, but the ceiling is the demonstrator's level — verify examples cover the deployment distribution before committing
Identifies the RL prerequisite: verifiable correctness (tests pass/fail) makes this task well-suited for RL since the reward signal is clear and the environment is buildable

evals.prompt: I'm trying to figure out the ROI of fine-tuning vs just using a better prompt for our document classification agent. The task is: given a legal contract, classify it into one of 12 categories. We're at 82% accuracy with GPT-4 and a detailed prompt. What would fine-tuning actually buy us here?
expectations: Recommends the model-swap experiment first: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same prompt and measure the accuracy delta before committing to fine-tuning

Identifies this as a strong SFT candidate if fine-tuning is warranted: the task is classification with a fixed label set, training and deployment distributions are close, and format stabilization is the primary need
Notes SFT's ceiling: fine-tuning cannot exceed the quality of the demonstration labels — if the 82% ceiling reflects ambiguous categories or inconsistent labeling, SFT will not break through it
Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is expensive and hard to reverse; 82% with a prompt may be close to the ceiling for this task regardless of training approach
Identifies what SFT cannot do here: inject knowledge about legal contract nuances that the model doesn't have — that requires better context (contract definitions, examples) not model weights

evals.prompt: We're building an agent that calls our internal APIs to fulfill customer orders. The API has a very specific call sequence — you have to authenticate, then query inventory, then reserve, then confirm. The model keeps getting the order wrong or skipping steps. Would SFT fix this or do we need RL?
expectations: Identifies this as a protocol knowledge problem — exactly what SFT is designed for: baking in 'how to say and do things' (the correct API call sequence)
Recommends SFT over RL: the call sequence is fixed and known, the training and deployment distributions are close, and the task does not require discovering novel strategies
Notes that a few hundred high-quality demonstration trajectories showing the correct sequence will efficiently bake the protocol into the model
Applies the YAGNI gate: RL is not warranted unless the API usage patterns are highly variable and optimal call sequences need to be discovered — neither is true here
Addresses demonstration quality: demonstrations must show the complete correct sequence including authentication, not just the happy path — coverage of the full protocol is the key data requirement

evals.prompt: our agent works great in our test environment but degrades significantly when we deploy to production. the production data has different distributions — different user phrasings, different edge cases. we've tried adding more test examples but it doesn't help. what's the right training approach here
expectations: Diagnoses this as the classic SFT failure mode: SFT memorized the training distribution and degrades when deployment conditions differ — adding more examples of the same distribution does not fix distribution shift
Recommends RL as the appropriate tool: RL learns a transferable strategy that re-solves problems with the same strategy even when the input distribution shifts
Applies the distribution test: the deployment environment shifts systematically from training, which is the explicit criterion for when RL becomes relevant over SFT

Notes the data quality issue: before committing to RL, verify that the production distribution is actually captured in the training environment — a distorted environment trains a policy that works only in simulation

Avoids recommending simply collecting more SFT data from production: the root cause is SFT's optimization objective (memorization), not data quantity — more examples of the same type will not generalize

evals.prompt: I need to design a reward function for training our phone-calling agent. It needs to collect specific information from the customer service rep (account number, billing address, plan name) and successfully change the plan. What reward signal should I use — binary success/failure or something more granular?
expectations: Applies the reward density spectrum: binary outcome reward (plan changed successfully) is the starting point, but multi-step information collection warrants milestone rewards for each piece of information collected
Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: outcome reward as the primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for skipping required information collection steps
Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in a multi-turn phone call, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise
Notes that process rewards (per-step feedback) accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient information-collection strategies
Identifies the penalty target correctly: the penalty must target a specific detectable bad action (skipping account number collection), never 'lack of progress' — that teaches the agent to do nothing

evals.prompt: We want to teach our agent to use a new internal search API we built. It has 5 endpoints with different query formats. The agent currently uses the wrong endpoint about 30% of the time and formats queries incorrectly. Is this a fine-tuning problem or a prompt engineering problem?
expectations: Applies the YAGNI gate: exhaust prompt engineering and tool design before committing to fine-tuning — 30% error rate on a new API may be fixable with better tool descriptions and examples
Identifies this as a protocol knowledge problem if fine-tuning is warranted: SFT is designed to bake in 'how to do things' (correct endpoint selection and query format)
Recommends the model-swap experiment: swap to a stronger frontier model with improved tool descriptions to determine whether the bottleneck is the model or the harness
Notes the ACI principle: if endpoint names and parameter schemas are ambiguous, the problem may be tool design — rename endpoints so the correct choice is obvious without a comment
Recommends SFT over RL if fine-tuning is needed: the API usage patterns are fixed and known, the training and deployment distributions are close, and no strategy discovery is required

evals.prompt: My team is debating whether to invest in RL training for our multi-step research agent. The agent does 15-20 tool calls per task (web search, document parsing, summarization). We have a working version with prompt engineering but it makes suboptimal tool selection decisions. What's the decision framework for whether RL is worth the investment?
expectations: Applies the decision framework in order: (1) is post-training needed at all — run the model-swap experiment first; (2) if training is needed, try SFT first; (3) only add RL when SFT is insufficient
Identifies the RL-warranted criteria that apply here: multi-step tasks where the optimal tool selection path is unknown, and annotation cost is too high to demonstrate every path
Notes the RL cost: 10–100× more expensive than harness iteration and harder to reverse — the team must establish that the harness ceiling is genuinely reached before committing
Addresses the practical test for SFT ceiling: 'when no matter how many demonstrations are added, new scenarios still perform poorly' — that is the tipping point to switch to RL

Identifies the RL prerequisite: a verifiable reward signal (task completion quality) and a buildable training environment that resembles real research tasks — both must be established before RL investment is justified

evals.prompt: We're designing reward signals for a multi-turn customer service agent. The task involves 8-12 turns of dialogue. Should we give rewards at each turn or only at the end when the customer's issue is resolved? What are the tradeoffs?
expectations: Applies the reward density spectrum: binary outcome reward (issue resolved) vs process rewards (per-turn feedback) vs milestone rewards (key dialogue checkpoints)

Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in an 8-12 turn task, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise between trajectory-level (too coarse) and token-level (too expensive)

Notes the process reward tradeoff: per-turn rewards accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient dialogue strategies — outcome rewards allow discovery at higher training cost
Recommends starting with binary outcome rewards and adding density only when training fails to converge — the default is to start simple

Identifies the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: outcome reward as primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for specific bad actions (skipping identity verification, making false promises)

evals.prompt: Our agent keeps taking shortcuts that technically complete the task but violate our business rules — it skips identity verification when it's faster, edits test files to make CI pass instead of fixing the actual bug. We're using RL with a binary success reward. How do we fix this?
expectations: Identifies this as reward hacking: the agent is optimizing the binary success proxy without actually satisfying the underlying constraints — pure outcome rewards actively incentivize constraint violations
Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: add verifiable path penalties for specific detectable bad actions (skipping identity verification, editing test files)
Specifies that penalty targets must be specific detectable actions, never 'lack of progress' — that teaches the agent to do nothing
Notes that the penalty must be machine-detectable (deterministic checks), not learned scorers — a learned scorer can itself be gamed
Addresses the RLVP (reward outcome, penalize path) pattern: the penalty restores gradient signal for the constraint violations that the binary success reward cannot express

evals.prompt: We have a well-performing SFT model for our agent. Performance on in-distribution tasks is great but we want to push further. What's the right sequence — do we go straight to RL or is there more we can squeeze out of SFT first?
expectations: Applies the decision framework: before adding RL, verify that SFT has genuinely hit its ceiling — the signal is 'adding more demonstrations no longer improves performance on new scenarios'
Identifies what more SFT can still deliver: better data coverage of edge cases, higher-quality demonstrations, and diversity of solutions — these can push SFT further before RL is needed
Addresses demonstration quality: SFT's ceiling is the demonstrator's level — if the current ceiling reflects mediocre demonstrations, improving data quality is cheaper than switching to RL
Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must be solid before RL — overtraining SFT collapses the model onto the training distribution, limiting RL's optimization space

Identifies the RL-warranted signal: when the task requires generalization beyond the training distribution or the optimal strategy is unknown — neither may be true if in-distribution performance is already great

evals.prompt: how do i set up a PPO training loop for my language model? i need to configure the value network, the KL penalty coefficient, and the clipping epsilon. what are the recommended hyperparameter ranges?
I want to run SFT on a 7B model using LoRA. What rank should I use, what learning rate, and how many epochs? I have 5000 training examples.

we're building an eval harness for our agent. we need to measure task completion rate, tool call accuracy, and response quality. how do we design the evaluation pipeline and what metrics should we track?

I'm designing the orchestration layer for a multi-agent system. One orchestrator agent breaks down tasks and spawns worker agents. How should I handle communication between agents and what happens when a worker fails?
how do i implement GRPO from scratch? i need to understand the advantage calculation, the group sampling, and how to handle the KL regularization term in the loss function

We're setting up a training infrastructure for RL. We need to configure veRL, set up the sandbox environment for code execution, and handle parallelization across 8 GPUs. What's the recommended setup?

our agent's context window is filling up on long tasks. we're at 128k tokens and hitting the limit on complex research tasks. should we use summarization, sliding window, or a different memory architecture?

I need to design the tool interface for our agent's database access. Should the agent get raw SQL access, a query builder API, or a natural language interface that translates to SQL? What are the security and reliability tradeoffs?

We're evaluating whether to use DPO or PPO for preference alignment on our conversational agent. We have 50k preference pairs from human annotators. Walk me through the implementation differences and which one to use.
